# Compound Interest

Compound interest is interest earned not only on the original amount but also on the
interest already accumulated. Because each period's interest is added to the balance
and then itself earns interest, savings grow faster and faster over time. This
snowball effect is why starting to save early matters far more than saving large
amounts later.

## The rule of 72

A handy shortcut, the rule of 72, estimates how long money takes to double: divide 72
by the annual percentage rate. At 6 percent, money roughly doubles every twelve
years. The same mathematics works against borrowers, because unpaid interest on debt
also compounds, which is how small balances on high-rate credit can grow alarmingly.
